generated_oml:
// C2: Acting Components
instance OccupancyActuator : DTDFVocab:ActingComponent [
    base:desc "Automated control of occupancy-based lighting and HVAC systems for energy efficiency"
]

instance HVACActuator : DTDFVocab:ActingComponent [
    base:desc "Dynamic adjustment of heating, ventilation, and air conditioning systems based on real-time environmental data"
]

instance LightingActuator : DTDFVocab:ActingComponent [
    base:desc "Automated control of lighting intensity and scheduling for optimal energy usage and occupant comfort"
]

// C3: Physical Sensing Components
instance IndoorTemperatureSensor : DTDFVocab:SensingComponent [
    base:desc "High-precision temperature sensor deployed across building spaces for indoor environmental monitoring"
    DTDFVocab:producedData TemperatureData
]

instance HumiditySensor : DTDFVocab:SensingComponent [
    base:desc "Relative humidity measurement device integrated into building infrastructure for environmental monitoring"
    DTDFVocab:producedData HumidityData
]

instance CO2Sensor : DTDFVocab:SensingComponent [
    base:desc "Carbon dioxide concentration sensor for air quality monitoring in indoor environments"
    DTDFVocab:producedData CO2Data
]

instance OccupancySensor : DTDFVocab:SensingComponent [
    base:desc "Occupancy detection system using PIR or motion sensors for space utilization tracking"
    DTDFVocab:producedData OccupancyData
]

instance EnergyConsumptionSensor : DTDFVocab:SensingComponent [
    base:desc "Real-time energy consumption monitoring for HVAC and lighting systems"
    DTDFVocab:producedData EnergyConsumptionData
]

instance ThermalComfortSensor : DTDFVocab:SensingComponent [
    base:desc "Comprehensive thermal comfort measurement including operative temperature and radiant temperature"
    DTDFVocab:producedData ThermalComfortData
]

// C4: Data Transmission
instance TemperatureData : DTDFVocab:DataTransmitted [
    DTDFVocab:producedFrom IndoorTemperatureSensor
]

instance HumidityData : DTDFVocab:DataTransmitted [
    DTDFVocab:producedFrom HumiditySensor
]

instance CO2Data : DTDFVocab:DataTransmitted [
    DTDFVocab:producedFrom CO2Sensor
]

instance OccupancyData : DTDFVocab:DataTransmitted [
    DTDFVocab:producedFrom OccupancySensor
]

instance EnergyConsumptionData : DTDFVocab:DataTransmitted [
    DTDFVocab:producedFrom EnergyConsumptionSensor
]

instance ThermalComfortData : DTDFVocab:DataTransmitted [
    DTDFVocab:producedFrom ThermalComfortSensor
]

// C10: Models/Data
instance BIMModel : DTDFVocab:Model [
    base:desc "Semantically rich 3D Building Information Model representing building components and spatial relationships"
    DTDFVocab:inputTo EclipseBasyxPlatform
]

instance OperationalModel : DTDFVocab:Model [
    base:desc "Runtime operational model derived from BIM with device configurations and control logic"
    DTDFVocab:inputTo ModelBasedSystemsEngineering
]

instance PredictiveAnalyticsModel : DTDFVocab:Model [
    base:desc "Time-series data processing model for forecasting energy consumption and equipment failure patterns"
    DTDFVocab:inputTo TensorFlowFramework
]

instance SimulationModel : DTDFVocab:Model [
    base:desc "Model-based engineering simulation for thermal and energy performance validation"
    DTDFVocab:inputTo EnergyPlusSimulation
]

instance RealTimeDataStore : DTDFVocab:Data [
    base:desc "Real-time data storage system for sensor readings and operational parameters"
    DTDFVocab:fromData TemperatureData, HumidityData, CO2Data, OccupancyData, EnergyConsumptionData, ThermalComfortData
    DTDFVocab:inputTo SQLDatabase
]

instance HistoricalDataStore : DTDFVocab:Data [
    base:desc "Historical data repository for trend analysis and performance benchmarking"
    DTDFVocab:fromData RealTimeDataStore
    DTDFVocab:inputTo SQLDatabase
]

instance GeospatialModel : DTDFVocab:Data [
    base:desc "3D spatial representation of building components for visualization and decision support"
    DTDFVocab:fromData BIMModel
    DTDFVocab:inputTo ITwinJSLibrary
]

// C11: Enablers
instance EclipseBasyxPlatform : DTDFVocab:Enabler [
    base:desc "Open-source industrial IoT platform for low-latency data processing and edge computing"
    DTDFVocab:enables RealTimeDataProcessingService
]

instance ITwinJSLibrary : DTDFVocab:Enabler [
    base:desc "JavaScript library for developing infrastructure digital twin applications with BIM integration"
    DTDFVocab:enables InteractiveVisualizationService
]

instance ModelBasedSystemsEngineering : DTDFVocab:Enabler [
    base:desc "System-level modeling framework for digital twin validation and behavior simulation"
    DTDFVocab:enables SimulationService
]

instance TensorFlowFramework : DTDFVocab:Enabler [
    base:desc "GPU-optimized machine learning framework for real-time predictive analytics"
    DTDFVocab:enables PredictiveAnalyticsService
]

instance EnergyPlusSimulation : DTDFVocab:Enabler [
    base:desc "Industry-standard thermal and energy simulation tool for building performance modeling"
    DTDFVocab:enables SimulationService
]

instance SQLDatabase : DTDFVocab:Enabler [
    base:desc "Relational database system for storing sensor data, model metadata, and historical trends"
    DTDFVocab:enables DataStorageService
]

instance GraphDatabase : DTDFVocab:Enabler [
    base:desc "Graph database for relationship-centric modeling of physical-digital interactions"
    DTDFVocab:enables GeospatialModel
]

// C6: Services
instance RealTimeDataProcessingService : DTDFVocab:Service [
    base:desc "Continuous acquisition and validation of physical conditions via sensor networks and edge computing"
    DTDFVocab:provides RealTimeDataInsight
]

instance PredictiveAnalyticsService : DTDFVocab:Service [
    base:desc "Forecasting trends using time-series data and machine learning models"
    DTDFVocab:provides PredictiveMaintenanceInsight
]

instance DynamicOptimizationService : DTDFVocab:Service [
    base:desc "Dynamic adjustments to HVAC, lighting, and occupancy systems for maximum efficiency"
    DTDFVocab:provides EnergyOptimizationAction
]

instance InteractiveVisualizationService : DTDFVocab:Service [
    base:desc "Interactive dashboards and 3D spatial modeling for decision-making support"
    DTDFVocab:provides VisualizationInsight
]

instance SimulationService : DTDFVocab:Service [
    base:desc "Model-based engineering simulation for validating physical behaviors"
    DTDFVocab:provides SimulationInsight
]

instance ComplianceReportingService : DTDFVocab:Service [
    base:desc "Automated generation of compliance reports for LEED certification"
    DTDFVocab:provides ComplianceReportAction
]

// C17: Insights/Actions
instance RealTimeDataInsight : DTDFVocab:Insight [
    base:desc "Dynamic state monitoring of building operations enabling proactive anomaly detection"
]

instance PredictiveMaintenanceInsight : DTDFVocab:Insight [
    base:desc "Alerts for potential equipment failures based on energy consumption patterns"
]

instance EnergyOptimizationAction : DTDFVocab:Action [
    base:desc "Automated control adjustments to HVAC and lighting systems for energy efficiency"
    DTDFVocab:IsAutomatic true
]

instance VisualizationInsight : DTDFVocab:Insight [
    base:desc "Interactive 3D spatial representation of building conditions"
]

instance SimulationInsight : DTDFVocab:Insight [
    base:desc "Predicted performance outcomes under different operational scenarios"
]

instance ComplianceReportAction : DTDFVocab:Action [
    base:desc "Automated generation of compliance documentation"
    DTDFVocab:IsAutomatic true
]

// C17: Environment
instance BuildingEnvironment : DTDFVocab:Environment [
    base:contains IndoorTemperatureSensor, HumiditySensor, CO2Sensor,
                 OccupancySensor, EnergyConsumptionSensor, ThermalComfortSensor,
                 HVACActuator, LightingActuator, OccupancyActuator,
                 BIMModel, OperationalModel, PredictiveAnalyticsModel,
                 SimulationModel, RealTimeDataStore, HistoricalDataStore,
                 GeospatialModel, EclipseBasyxPlatform, ITwinJSLibrary
]

instance AcademicInfrastructureEnvironment : DTDFVocab:Environment [
    base:contains BuildingEnvironment,
                 LEEDComplianceModel,
                 ResearchUseCaseModel
]

instance LEEDComplianceModel : DTDFVocab:Component [
    base:desc "LEED certification-specific model tracking compliance metrics"
]

instance ResearchUseCaseModel : DTDFVocab:Component [
    base:desc "Academic research-oriented model supporting iterative development"
]

instance virtual_to_physical_interaction : DTDFVocab:VirtualToPhysical [
    base:desc "
The **virtual_to_physical_interaction** mechanism enables **real-time control commands** from the digital twin to physical systems, facilitated by the DT Framework’s bidirectional data exchange capabilities. Command types include operational adjustments (e.g., HVAC system modulation, lighting control) derived from **facility management interactions** by **Facility Managers**. Control message formats are likely standardized through the **modular architecture’s extension points**, ensuring interoperability with legacy and new systems.

Validation and authentication mechanisms are implied by the **requirements-driven approach**, emphasizing **interoperability** and **scalability**. Feedback systems are enabled via **immersive visualization interfaces**, allowing immediate confirmation of command execution (e.g., system acknowledgments, error logs). Conflict resolution is handled through **runtime configuration management**, where adjustments are made dynamically without redeployment, ensuring system stability. No explicit mention of encryption (e.g., TLS) or authorization protocols (e.g., OAuth) is provided, but the framework’s **non-functional requirements (NF)** suggest robust security measures are implied.
"
]

instance twinning_time_scale : DTDFVocab:TimeScale [
    base:desc "
The Digital Twin system employs **real-time data exchange capabilities** to ensure **continuous synchronization** between physical buildings and their digital counterparts, with no explicit latency or frequency specifications mentioned. The framework supports **runtime configuration management**, enabling dynamic adjustments to synchronization parameters without redeployment, though the exact granularity of temporal alignment (e.g., millisecond, second-level updates) is not detailed. The system leverages **BIM (Building Information Modeling) tools** for initial device modeling, implying a foundational alignment with operational cycles of building systems (e.g., HVAC, lighting), but no granularity thresholds are specified beyond 'continuous' synchronization. The absence of explicit time-series data handling (e.g., IoT sensors with predefined update intervals) suggests a flexible, adaptive approach to temporal fidelity, though the documents do not quantify the upper bounds for latency or service-specific time scales (e.g., predictive analytics vs. real-time control)."
]

instance dt_constellation : DTDFVocab:Constellation [
    base:desc "
The Digital Twin system’s **orchestration** is governed by a **centralized architecture pattern** with the **DT Framework** as the core hub, enabling **bidirectional data exchange** between physical buildings and their digital counterparts. Key components include:
1. **Enriched BIM models** (input layer) transformed into operational DTs via BIM tools.
2. **Role-based contributions**:
   - **Building Control Engineers** handle device modeling.
   - **Facility Managers** interact with real-time monitoring/control interfaces.
   - **DT Engineers** integrate custom services/analytics via standardized extension points.
3. **Resource management** is implicit through modularity and runtime configuration, allowing low-overhead updates without full redeployment.
4. **System-wide coordination** is achieved via the central hub’s role in facilitating **holistic building life-cycle management**, though no explicit orchestration protocols (e.g., service-oriented architecture, event-driven models) or failure recovery mechanisms are detailed. The framework’s **coordination mechanisms** rely on interoperability standards (e.g., for BIM-to-DT data flow) but lack specifics on latency thresholds or conflict resolution for concurrent operations. The absence of mention of **distributed coordination** (e.g., blockchain, consensus-based twins) suggests a centralized approach, though the documents do not specify scalability limits for the constellation size."
]

instance life_cycle_stages : DTDFVocab:EvolutionStage [
    base:desc "
The lifecycle stages of the Digital Twin (DT) system span from **initial device modeling to runtime custom service development**, addressing the fundamental gap between BIM’s static representations and DTs' dynamic nature. The framework supports a **complete life cycle** encompassing:

1. **Device Modeling Phase**:
   - **Representation**: Building Control Engineers (BCEs) model Building and Control Systems (BCS) components directly within BIM environments, explicitly defining **sensors, actuators, and controllers** to overcome BIM’s limitations in representing dynamic BCS elements.
   - **Methodology**: Enriched BIM models leverage **Model-Driven Engineering (MDE)** principles to extract structured representations, ensuring a unified semantic foundation for subsequent transformations.

2. **Transformation Phase**:
   - **Artifact Generation**: Automated extraction of deployable DT artifacts via MDE, minimizing manual intervention to streamline deployment workflows.

3. **Runtime Phase**:
   - **Real-Time Synchronization**: Continuous bidirectional data exchange between physical buildings and their digital counterparts, enabling **real-time state monitoring** and **operational control** via the DT.
   - **Visualization & Interaction**: Immersive interfaces for **interactive visualization**, facilitating Facility Managers’ (FMs) monitoring and control of physical infrastructure through the DT.
   - **Extension & Evolution**: Modular architecture with **standardized extension points** supports integration of **custom services and analytics**, allowing DTs to adapt to evolving operational requirements.

**Lifecycle Governance**:
   - The framework integrates **version control** implicitly through MDE-driven artifact generation, ensuring traceability and reproducibility of DT configurations.
   - **Lifecycle Use Cases** (Table II) explicitly outline stages from device modeling to custom service development, aligning with stakeholder roles (BCEs, FMs, DT Engineers) and operational needs.
"
]

instance fidelity_and_validity_considerations : DTDFVocab:FidelityConsideration [
    base:desc "
The Digital Twin system prioritizes **semantic integration, real-time connectivity, and extensibility** to ensure high-fidelity alignment between BIM-based models and operational data. The **model-driven automation framework** (as per **R2**) ensures reproducible DT deployments, reducing discrepancies between design-time and runtime models. While validation and verification methods are not explicitly detailed, the system’s reliance on **Eclipse Hono/Ditto** for live synchronization implies stringent data synchronization protocols to mitigate latency and ensure accuracy. The goal is to operationalize DTs in AECO projects by addressing **scalable DT engineering**, implying robust error detection and correction mechanisms for model fidelity. Uncertainty quantification is implied through the integration of **BIM’s collaborative frameworks** and real-time operational feedback loops.
"
]

instance dt_technical_connection : DTDFVocab:TechnicalConnection [
    base:desc "
The **dt_technical_connection** infrastructure is designed for **scalable, heterogeneous system integration**, leveraging a **centralized DT Framework** as the core hub. Communication protocols are not explicitly named but are inferred to support **real-time synchronization** and **low-overhead updates**, likely using protocols like **MQTT, WebSockets, or proprietary DT-specific protocols** for bidirectional data exchange.

Network topology and architecture are modular, allowing **extension points** for custom services and analytics. Bandwidth and latency requirements are implied by the need for **real-time monitoring and control**, though exact specifications (e.g., Mbps, ms latency) are not provided. Reliability and redundancy mechanisms are supported by **runtime configuration management**, enabling dynamic adjustments to maintain system availability. Security protocols are not detailed but are implied by the **non-functional requirements (NF)**, which emphasize **interoperability across heterogeneous systems**, suggesting encryption (e.g., TLS), access controls, and fault tolerance.
"
]

instance dt_hosting_deployment : DTDFVocab:Deployment [
    base:desc "
The **dt_hosting_deployment** infrastructure supports **cloud/edge/on-premise** deployment models, with a focus on **scalability** and **low-overhead updates**. The framework’s **modular architecture** enables deployment on **computational and storage platforms** that can scale from individual components to entire infrastructure systems, ensuring adaptability to operational needs.

Computational requirements are not specified, but the emphasis on **runtime configuration management** suggests support for **dynamic workload balancing** and **distributed processing**. Storage needs are implied to be optimized for **BIM model enrichment** and **real-time data retention**, with **availability/reliability assurance** ensured through the **centralized DT Framework**. Deployment models supported include **incremental updates** (via runtime adjustments) and **service integration** by **DT Engineers**, avoiding full redeployment. No explicit mention of cloud providers (e.g., AWS, Azure) or edge computing frameworks (e.g., Kubernetes) is provided, but the framework’s **non-functional requirements (NF)** suggest cloud/edge/on-premise flexibility.
"
]

instance horizontal_integration : DTDFVocab:HoriIntegration [
    base:desc "
The Digital Twin system integrates with **external systems** through:
1. **Data exchange protocols**: Implicitly relies on **BIM tools** (e.g., Revit, ArchiCAD) for device modeling and deployment, enabling interoperability with legacy AECO (Architecture, Engineering, Construction, Operations) systems via standardized extension points.
2. **Interoperability standards**: The framework bridges the gap between **BIM’s static representations** and dynamic operational needs, implying adherence to AECO industry standards (e.g., IFC, COBie) for data exchange, though no specific protocols (e.g., REST APIs, MQTT) or compliance certifications are mentioned.
3. **Dependencies**: External systems include **BIM tools** (for initial modeling) and **facility management platforms** (for real-time monitoring), with no explicit dependencies on third-party IoT gateways or cloud services. The system supports **integration patterns** such as:
   - **Service integration** via DT Engineers.
   - **Bidirectional data flow** between physical and digital twins.
   However, no details are provided on integration patterns with **other Digital Twins** (e.g., cross-building or enterprise-wide DT constellations) or enterprise systems (e.g., ERP, CMMS). The modular architecture allows for **custom service extensions**, but the documents do not specify interoperability constraints (e.g., data format conversions, security protocols) or performance benchmarks for external system coupling."
]

instance data_ownership_and_privacy : DTDFVocab:DataOwnershipPrivacy [
    base:desc "Within the context of **Architecture, Engineering, and Construction (AECO)** Digital Twin (DT) systems, the provided documents do not explicitly detail **data ownership policies**, **privacy protection measures**, **compliance with data protection regulations**, **consent management**, **data anonymization**, or **data sharing agreements**. However, the emphasis on **BIM (Building Information Modeling)** integration—particularly in studies like Gerber et al.’s Living Lab for a LEED Platinum academic building—suggests that data governance must align with **AECO industry standards** (e.g., ISO 19650 for BIM collaboration). While proprietary ecosystems (e.g., Bentley’s iTwin.js) may implicitly enforce ownership through vendor-controlled workflows, the lack of **Model-Driven Engineering (MDE)** abstraction in surveyed works implies **manual compliance** with privacy laws (e.g., GDPR) is often required. The absence of explicit mention of **MDE-driven data governance frameworks** (e.g., ontological models for consent tracking) leaves AECO DTs vulnerable to **semantic drift** (Abbasi et al.), where ownership ambiguities arise from misaligned physical-digital mappings. Thus, while **data anonymization** may be implied in real-time IoT-BIM integration (Opoku et al.), no concrete protocols (e.g., federated learning for privacy-preserving analytics) are documented. **Not in Document** applies to all ownership/privacy-specific elements due to the documents' focus on technical integration rather than governance policies."
]

instance standardization : DTDFVocab:Standardization [
    base:desc "
The Digital Twin framework adheres to **industry-specific standards** and frameworks to ensure interoperability and compliance, though no explicit certification requirements or detailed specifications are provided. Key aspects include:

1. **Industry Standards & Specifications**:
   - **BIM Compliance**: Leverages **BIM standards** (e.g., IFC, COBIE) to model BCS components, ensuring compatibility with existing building information infrastructure.
   - **MDE Principles**: Follows **Model-Driven Engineering (MDE) standards** (e.g., OMG’s MOF, UML) for structured artifact generation, though no specific vendor or version is cited.
   - **Real-Time Protocols**: Implicitly assumes compliance with **industry protocols** for data exchange (e.g., OPC UA, MQTT) for synchronization, though not named.

2. **Interoperability Standards**:
   - **Standardized Extension Points**: Modular architecture supports **plug-and-play integration** of custom services, aligning with **open APIs** (e.g., REST, gRPC) for interoperability.
   - **Semantic Alignment**: Unified semantic foundation from BIM ensures **cross-system compatibility** between physical infrastructure and DT artifacts.

3. **Compliance Frameworks**:
   - **Lifecycle Alignment**: Use cases (Table II) and stakeholder roles (Table I) imply compliance with **ISO/IEC 27001 (security)**, **ISO 41001 (facility management)**, and **IEC 62443 (industrial cybersecurity)** implicitly, though not explicitly referenced.
   - **No Formal Certification**: No mention of third-party certifications (e.g., ISO 19650 for BIM) or compliance audits is provided.

**Note**: The framework’s reliance on **BIM and MDE** suggests alignment with **European Union (EU) digital twin initiatives** (e.g., Horizon Europe), though this is inferred rather than stated.
"
]

instance security_and_safety_considerations : DTDFVocab:SecuritySafety [
    base:desc "The provided documents highlight **security and safety considerations** in AECO Digital Twin (DT) systems primarily through **runtime adaptability** (Lehner et al.), **semantic coherence** (Kamburjan et al.), and **tool interoperability** (e.g., BIM-IoT integration). Key technical aspects include:
  - **Cybersecurity protocols**: Implicit reliance on **proprietary ecosystems** (e.g., Bentley’s iTwin.js) for authentication and access control, though no explicit mention of **zero-trust architectures** or **end-to-end encryption** (e.g., TLS 1.3) is documented.
  - **Access control mechanisms**: Assumed to be enforced via **BIM collaboration frameworks** (ISO 19650) or **role-based access control (RBAC)** in custom-built solutions, but no granular specifications (e.g., multi-factor authentication for DT gateways) are provided.
  - **Threat mitigation strategies**: Addressed through **synchronization techniques** to combat **semantic drift** (Abbasi et al.), where misalignment between physical and digital models introduces vulnerabilities. Declarative life-cycle languages (Kamburjan et al.) may enable **rule-based threat detection**, but no concrete examples (e.g., AI-driven anomaly detection) are cited.
  - **Fail-safe mechanisms**: Implied in **model-driven governance** (MDE), where formalized policies (e.g., ontological frameworks, Oakes et al.) ensure compliance with safety-critical systems (e.g., emergency response simulations in LEED buildings). However, no details on **fail-safe validation** (e.g., redundancy checks in DT runtime) are documented.
  - **Risk assessment approaches**: Focused on **data integration challenges** (e.g., stakeholder coordination failures) rather than cybersecurity risks. The **surveyed works** (Gerber et al., Opoku et al.) do not address **quantitative risk modeling** (e.g., NIST frameworks) for DT systems.
  - **Safety-critical system considerations**: Explicitly tied to **real-time performance analysis** (e.g., indoor environmental optimization in libraries) and **simulation validation** (BIM-IoT DTs), but no mention of **hardware-in-the-loop (HIL) testing** or **real-time safety protocols** (e.g., IoT-based intrusion detection) is provided.

**Not in Document** applies to explicit security protocols, fail-safes, and risk assessment methodologies due to the documents’ emphasis on **model-driven integration** and **data synchronization** over cybersecurity governance."
]
